segments.s497-g153: بسی چیز بخشید و نستد کسی — The phrase is past-tense and poetic in order: object + verb. بسی چیز = “many/much (things)” (an adverbial/quantifying phrase placed before the verb). بخشید is the past of بخشیدن ‘to give, bestow’. و links the two past actions. نستد is the past (classical) negative sense of ستادن ‘to take/exact’, here meaning “did not exact/take”; کسی with the negated verb functions as ‘anyone’ (i.e. ‘from anyone’ none was exacted).
segments.s497-g154: نبد آز نزدیک ایشان بسی — نبد is an archaic contraction of نبود ‘there was not’ (existential negative). آز = ‘greed/avarice’. نزدیک ایشان = ‘near them / in their vicinity’. بسی at the end is an intensifier meaning ‘much/very’ and here limits the predicate: literally “there was not (much) greed near them,” i.e. they were not greatly greedy.
segments.s497-g155: بی‌آزار ازان جایگه برگرفت — بی‌آزار is an adverbial phrase ‘without harming / harmlessly’ modifying the verb. ازان جایگه = ‘from that place’ (از + ان + جایگه). برگرفت is a past verb here meaning ‘withdrew / removed himself from’ (literally ‘took away’), so the whole clause means he departed from that place without causing harm.
segments.s497-g156: بران هم نشان راه خاور گرفت — بران (‘thereupon’/‘upon that’) links this action to the previous one. هم adds the sense ‘also/then’. نشان گرفت is an idiom here: نشان (a sign, standard) + گرفت (took/raised) = ‘he set up/raised his sign/standard’. راه خاور گرفت = ‘he took the road to the east’ (راه = road/path, خاور = east, گرفت = ‘took’). The verb گرفت applies to these coordinated results.
segments.s497-g81: Word-for-word order is subject + adverb + verb + object + indirect object: 'برهمن' (the Brahmin) is the subject, 'چنین' ('thus') modifies the verb, 'داد' is the past tense 'gave', and 'پاسخ به شاه' is the direct object plus recipient ('an answer to the king'). The verb داد is simple past and governs the following noun 'پاسخ'.
segments.s497-g82: This is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' = 'that'. Word order here treats 'آب را' (water + definite/object marker را) as the object of the following predicate: 'خاک دارد نگاه' literally 'the earth has a look' — i.e. 'the earth casts/holds a gaze (on the water)'. 'هم' before 'آب' adds 'even/also'. 'دارد' is present-tense 'has' used with the noun 'نگاه' (gaze) to form the verbal idea 'casts a look'.
segments.s497-g83: 'بپرسید' is past third-person ('he asked'). The relativizer 'کز' here functions like 'who/which (is) from' and combines with 'خواب بیدار' to mean 'who is awake from sleep' (word order: verb-subject complement is compacted into the clause). 'کی ست' is the copula question 'who is'.
segments.s497-g84: 'به روی زمین' is the locative phrase 'on the face of the earth'. 'بر گنهکار کیست' is an interrogative clause: 'کیست' = 'who is', with 'بر گنهکار' indicating the predicate 'a sinner' (classical word order puts the predicate before the copula). Together the line asks, rhetorically, 'who on earth is a sinner?'.
segments.s497-g85: The clause begins with 'که' = 'that' introducing explanation. 'جنبندگانند' is a nominal/predicative plural meaning 'they are living beings' (classical Persian often uses such an -ند ending). 'و' = 'and'; 'چندی زیند' means 'they live for a while' — 'چندی' = 'for a time' and 'زیند' is the present/subjunctive-form of 'to live' in older spelling (here 'they live').
segments.s497-g86: 'ندانند' is the negative present/past-tense third-person 'they do not know'. 'کاندر' (classical fused form of 'کَه + در' / 'کَـ اندَر') functions as 'what/in which' referring to the events of the world; 'جهان' = 'the world'; 'برچیند' (from برچیدن) here is used poetically for 'what will come to pass / what will be done/what will befall' — the verb evokes 'taking away' or 'bringing about'. The whole clause: 'they do not know what the world will do to them.'
segments.s497-g87: Same construction as before: 'برهمن' (the Brahmin) + 'چنین' ('thus') + 'داد' (gave, past) + 'پاسخ' (an answer). 'بدوی' follows as an adjective/adverb meaning 'original / at first / plain' describing the manner or character of the answer; word order places the qualifier after the noun in classical style.
segments.s497-g88: This is a vocative/direct address introduced by 'که‌ای' (classical combination where 'که' carries connective sense and 'ای' marks address). 'پاکدل' = 'pure‑hearted' (an epithet), 'مهتر' = 'chief/elder', and 'راست گوی' = 'truth‑sayer' or 'one who speaks the truth' (compound epithet where 'راست' 'true' modifies 'گوی' 'speaker/sayer'). The phrase is an exclamation/appellation directed to the addressee.
segments.s497-g25: نبینی is the negative 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of دیدن (ن + بینی) used here to express 'you will not see' or 'you see not' as the main verb. جز از functions as an excluding phrase 'except for' (جز + از), and برهنه یک رمه is a noun phrase with the adjective برهنه preceding the numeral/indefinite یک and the noun رمه: 'a single bare flock.'
segments.s497-g26: پراگنده is an adjective 'scattered'; از here works as a preposition 'from/by' linking the scattering to its cause; روزگار in this context reads as 'fate/time'; دمه is 'moment' (literary 'breath'/'instant'), so the phrase means 'scattered by the moment/breath of time/fate.'
segments.s497-g27: اگر introduces a conditional. بودن is the infinitive nominal 'being' (your stay/existence); ایدر is an archaic/formal variant of 'here'; دراز is an adjective 'long' describing that being; آیدت is a 3rd-person-singular verb 'it comes' with the 2nd-person enclitic -ت meaning 'it comes to you / befalls you' — together 'if being here becomes long for you.'
segments.s497-g28: به introduces the thing wanted/needed ('for' or 'to'); تخم گیاها is the object 'seeds of plants' (تخم 'seed' + گیاها plural 'plants'); نیاز آیدت is an idiomatic verbless/fused predicate 'need will come to you' (آیدت = 'it comes to you'), i.e. 'you will need ...'.
segments.s497-g29: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause 'a messenger came'; بر شهریار uses بر with the sense 'to/toward' (بر + indirect object) giving 'came to the king.' Subject (فرستاده) precedes the verb, and شهریار is the indirect object introduced by بر.
segments.s497-g30: This clause is verbless/elliptical: ز بیخ گیا = 'from the root of the plant' (ز = from, بیخ = root, گیا = plant). بر میانش ازار = 'a pain/annoyance on his middle' (بر = on/upon, میانش = میان + enclitic 'his' = his middle/abdomen, ازار = harm/pain). The clause describes the suffering (pain in his middle caused by the plant's root).
segments.s497-g31: سکندر is the subject ('Alexander'); فرستاده و نامه are coordinated direct objects ('the messenger and the letter'); دید is the past-tense verb 'saw/beheld' placed at the end as typical in Persian main clauses.
segments.s497-g32: بی‌آزاری ('without-harm') and رامشی ('tranquility/tameness') form a coordinated object, and برگزید is the past transitive verb 'he chose/selected' governing them. The prefix بی- gives a negational/privative sense to آزار here resulting in 'harmlessness/clemency.'
segments.s497-g65: چنو is a temporal/adverbial particle meaning 'as/when' (classical usage). بگذرد is the verb ('to pass, depart') in a non-past/optative/classical form used here to indicate the event 'passes away'. زین سرای سپنج is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'from this house/abode ...' (زین = 'from this', سرای = 'house/abode') placed after the verb in poetic word order; سپنج is a modifier of سرای. The whole segment sets up the temporal clause 'when he passes from this dwelling.'
segments.s497-g66: ازو is a prepositional/pronominal phrase 'from him/of him'. بازماند is a past/static verb meaning 'remained, was left behind'. زر و تاج و گنج are a simple coordinated list of nouns ('gold and crown and treasure') serving as the thing that remains. The sequence states the result of the preceding temporal clause: 'from him remained ...'.
segments.s497-g67: چنان دان is an imperative construction: چنان ('thus/so') + دان (imperative of 'to know/consider') = 'consider it so' or 'think thus'. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. نیکیست همراه اوی functions as that subordinate clause: 'no goodness accompanies him' / 'goodness is not with him' (همراه اوی = 'with him' or 'his companion'). Grammatically, the verb/statement about 'nیکی' completes the command 'consider'.
segments.s497-g68: به خاک اندر آید is a verbal phrase 'comes into the dust/earth' (به ... اندر آید = 'falls into / comes into the earth'). سر و گاه اوی are the compound objects 'his head and his time' (سر = 'head', گاه = 'time/occasion'), with اوی a possessive reference 'his'. Note the classical poetic order: verb phrase precedes the objects here, but they are understood as the things that 'come into the dust.'
segments.s497-g69: سکندر is the subject name 'Alexander'. بپرسید is the past-tense verb 'asked' (classical past form). که کاندر جهان combines the subordinating که ('that/whether') with کاندر ('in/within') and جهان ('the world') to introduce the content of his question: 'that/in which in the world ...'.
segments.s497-g70: فزون آشکارا places the comparative/adverbial modifier up front: فزون ('more, greater') + آشکارا ('openly/evidently') = 'more openly/apparently'. بود‌گر is a classical conditional/correlative particle 'would be / if it were' (بود + گر). نهان is the opposing predicate 'hidden/secret'. Together the phrase asks 'was it more apparent or (was it) hidden?'.
segments.s497-g71: همان opens as a demonstrative 'that/the same'. زنده بیش است is a comparative statement 'is more (valuable) alive' (زنده = 'alive', بیش = 'more', است = 'is'). ‌گر introduces a concessive/conditional 'if', so است‌گر مرده نیز reads 'if (he) is dead as well'—مرده is 'dead', نیز adds 'also/too'. The segment contrasts the state of being alive with being dead.
segments.s497-g72: کزان پس is a causal/temporal link 'from that / therefore afterward'. نیازش is the possessed noun 'his need/supplication' (نیاز + -ش). نیاید is negative 'does not come' or 'will not arise'. به چیز 'for anything' completes the negation ('need for anything'). Together: 'thereafter his need for anything will not arise.'
segments.s497-g113: The clause has an omitted subject (third person singular) and a compound object phrase coordinated by و. دو رخ (lit. “two cheeks”) = two cheeks; رخ (here ‘cheek’) is followed by the adjective زرد (pale). دیده پر از آب is a noun phrase ‘eye(s) full of water’ with پر از «full of» + noun. کرد is the past tense verb (‘made/caused’) taking the preceding coordinated noun phrases as its complements (i.e. ‘made the cheeks pale and made the eyes full of water’).
segments.s497-g114: همان چهر خندان is a noun phrase ‘that same smiling face’ (همان = ‘the same/that’ + چهر ‘face’ + خندان ‘smiling’). پر از تاب کرد uses the pattern ‘پر از X کردن’ = ‘to fill (something) with X’ or ‘to make (something) full of X’; تاب here means radiance/brightness and کرد is again the past tense verb ‘made/caused’ (so: ‘he made that smiling face full of radiance’).
segments.s497-g115: بپرسید is a narrative past 3rd-person form (‘he asked’ / ‘he enquired’) with the archaic narrative prefix ب‑ found throughout Shahnameh; پس = ‘then/afterwards’; شاه فرمانروا is the addressed title ‘the sovereign king’ (شاه + appositive فرمانروا). Word order is subject/title after the verb, typical in epic narration.
segments.s497-g116: This is an indirect question introduced by که. حاجت چه باشد = ‘what need may there be’ (حاجت = ‘need/request’; چه = ‘what’). باشد is the subjunctive/present form of بودن used in indirect questions (‘may be / is’). شما را به ما literally orders the arguments: شما را = the experiencer/possessor ‘you’ marked by the object marker را, and به ما = ‘toward us / of us’ (what is your need concerning us). The whole phrase means ‘what is your need of us?’
segments.s497-g117: ندارم is the present negative first-person form ‘I do not have’ (here idiomatically ‘I will not withhold’). دریغ از شما = ‘reluctance/hesitation concerning you’ (دریغ is a noun; از introduces its object/target). گنج خویش is a possessive noun phrase ‘my/one's own treasure’ formed by گنج + خوی + enclitic ش (خویش = ‘one's own’); the enclitic ش marks the possessive/reflexive. The whole clause expresses readiness to give the treasure without hesitation.
segments.s497-g118: نه هرگز is a fronted negative adverbial ‘never / by no means’. براندیشم is first-person-singular subjunctive/present of اندیشیدن with the verbal prefix بر‑ (here ‘I will consider / I will give thought’), used with نه هرگز to mean ‘I shall never consider’. از رنج خویش = ‘about/of my own suffering’ (از = ‘about/of’; رنج = ‘suffering’; خویش = reflexive/possessive ‘one’s own’ formed with خوی + ش).
segments.s497-g119: بگفتند is the narrative past third-person plural ‘they said’ (with the narrative prefix ب‑). کای is a vocative particle ‘O’ (common in epic/formal addresses). شهریار بلند = ‘lofty/illustrious king’ (شهریار = king, بلند = lofty/lofty/lofty in status); adjective follows the noun.
segments.s497-g120: در مرگ و پیری = ‘in (your) death and old age’ (در = ‘in’, مرگ = ‘death’, پیری = ‘old age’). تو is the vocative/subject pronoun ‘you’. بر ما ببند is an imperative clause with ببند (‘close/shut’) and بر ما = ‘against us / toward us’ (i.e. ‘do not shut [your door] so that it excludes us’ in context: a plea regarding treatment in the king’s death/old age). The imperative form and ordering give a direct address/request to the king.
segments.s497-g137: پیامست is a poetic/hortative form meaning 'is a sign/portends' (here used as 'portends'); از introduces the theme 'of' or 'concerning' (پیامست از مرگ = 'a sign of death'); مرگ 'death' is the noun governed by از; موی سپید is a noun phrase 'white hair' (موی = hair/lock, سپید = white/white‑haired) placed after مرگ to show what the portent concerns.
segments.s497-g138: به بودن is an adverbial infinitive phrase 'as to being/while being, in being'; چه داری تو is a rhetorical present interrogative 'what do you have?' with the verb داری (present 'have') and the emphatic/poetic subject pronoun تو placed after the verb; چندین امید follows as the object 'many hopes' – overall a rhetorical question: 'what hopes do you (still) have of being alive?'.
segments.s497-g139: چنین گفت is a fixed formula meaning 'thus said' (چنین + گفت, with گفت used here as the past narrative verb 'said'); بیداردل is a compound adjective 'wakeful‑hearted' describing شهریار, which here means 'the king' (شهریار) — the adjective follows the verb cluster and precedes the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s497-g140: که‌گر is the conditional conjunction 'if' (classical/poetic form of اگر), introducing a subordinate conditional clause; بنده 'servant' is the subject, از بخشش کردگار is a genitival/agentive phrase 'by/from the bounty/grant of the Creator' (از marks the source), so the clause begins a conditional: 'if a servant, by the Creator's bounty...' (the remainder of the clause continues in the next sentence).
segments.s497-g141: گذر یافتی is an inverted conditional protasis: یافتی (2nd‑person past/conditional 'you had found/obtained') governs the noun گذر ('passage' or 'pass'); بودمی is an archaic past‑conditional form ('I would have been' / 'I had been') where the clitic -می signals the subjunctive/conditional nuance; من همان follows for emphasis: 'I (would have been) the same.' The inversion and archaic verb بودمی are typical of poetic conditional clauses.
segments.s497-g142: به تدبیر is a prepositional phrase 'by the counsel/plan'; بر گشتن is a verbal noun/infinitive construction 'the turning' (گشتن = 'to turn') with the preposition بر marking motion/turning over, and آسمان 'the sky/Heaven' is the agent/scene: together 'by the counsel of the turning Heaven' or 'by Heaven's deliberate turning' — a poetic expression for a heavenly decree or change of fate.
segments.s497-g143: که introduces a clause meaning 'for' or 'that' (giving a reason or explanation); فرزانه 'the wise' and مرد پرخاشخر 'the man who is quarrelsome/aggressive' are coordinated subjects (the adjective پرخاشخر follows مرد, forming 'mard‑e por‑khashakhar' = 'the quarrelsome man'). The clause states a general truth about these two kinds of people.
segments.s497-g144: ز بخشش 'from/by bounty' (ز marks source or means); به کوشش 'by effort/through striving' is an instrumental phrase; نیابد is a classical negative present/optative form meaning 'does not attain / cannot obtain' (third‑person singular negative), and گذر 'passage' is the object placed at the end. The whole means 'cannot, by effort, attain passage from bounty'.
segments.s497-g145: دگر here (= 'the other' / 'else') modifies هرک ('whoever'), producing 'whoever else'. The clause ends with the passive verb phrase کشته شد ('was killed'), with در جنگ من ('in my battle(s)') as a locative complement; Persian places the verb at the end and uses شد as the auxiliary forming the passive.
segments.s497-g146: کرا is an archaic relative pronoun ('who/whom') referring back to the slain person; the construction ز اخترش روز برگشته شد is literal 'from his star the day turned/was turned'—an idiom meaning his luck or allotted day had been turned away. برگشته شد uses برگشته (turned/returned) plus شد as auxiliary to mark the resulting state.
segments.s497-g147: به درد و به خون ریختن is a compound prepositional/gerundial phrase meaning 'to (suffer) pain and to the shedding of blood' (ریختن as a verbal noun/infinitive 'shedding'). بد سزا is the predicative noun phrase 'a bad/evil punishment/fate' — adjective follows the noun in this ordering.
segments.s497-g148: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason: 'that the oppressor spares no one.' Word order is somewhat inverted: کس (anyone/no one) + نیابد ('does not find' / 'spares') + رها ('free/spared') — literally 'the tyrant finds no one released', i.e. 'the tyrant spares no one.'
segments.s497-g149: بدیدند is a past‑tense plural verb 'they saw/experienced'. بادافره ایزدی is a nominal phrase: بادافره ('wind‑borne, changeable') qualified by ایزدی ('divine, god‑sent'), functioning as the object/experience they underwent.
segments.s497-g150: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; گشتند باز is the verb phrase 'they turned back' (گشتند = 'they turned'), and از ره بخردی is a prepositional phrase 'from the way/path of wisdom' (ره = 'way', بخردی = 'wisdom'), giving the source they turned away from.
segments.s497-g151: کس is indefinite/negative here ('no one'); از خواست یزدان is a genitive phrase 'from the will/desire of God' (خواست = 'will/desire'); کرانه نیافت means 'did not find a boundary/limit' — word order places the object (کرانه) before نیافت ('did not find').
segments.s497-g152: ز کار زمانه is 'in/from the affairs of time/fortune' (ز = 'from/of', کار زمانه = 'the business/working of fate'); بهانه نیافت repeats the verb phrase 'did not find an excuse/pretext', tying back to کس ('no one'), so the clause means 'nor could anyone find a pretext in the workings of fate.'
segments.s497-g49: همه خوردنیشان is a noun phrase: همه 'all' quantifies خوردنی‑شان (خوردنی + possessive -شان) meaning 'all their edibles/food'. بر + میوه‌دار is a locative/prepositional phrase 'on/onto the fruit‑bearing (plants)'; the verb 'was' is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the phrase functions as the predicate of origin/place.
segments.s497-g50: ز تخم گیا رسته is a chained prepositional/agentive phrase: ز 'from' + تخم 'seed' + گیا 'plant' (genitive relationship) followed by رسته, a past‑participle/adjective 'sprung/grown' that modifies the preceding idea. بر کوهسار is a second locative 'on the mountain‑slope(s)'. The word order (source + participle + place) is typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s497-g51: This clause has an allocative/nominal preface (ازار) followed by numeric modifier یکی 'one/a single' + noun چرم نخچیر 'leather of game/prey', and ends with بود 'was' (past copula). The structure is [prepositional/ topical word] + [quantity + NP] + بود, i.e. 'As for X, one piece of game leather was (their)...'.
segments.s497-g52: گیا پوشش 'plants (g vegetation) as covering' is a nominal phrase (گیا = plant, پوشش = covering). The conjunction و links the next clause: خوردن آژیر بود, where خوردن here functions nominally ('food, that which is eaten') and is followed by آژیر (a noun whose precise referent is lexical/archaic here) and بود 'was'. The verb 'بود' again is the past copula; the clause lists coverings and foods.
segments.s497-g53: سکندر is the subject. بپرسیدش is the past/subjunctive‑flavored verb 'asked him' (بپرسید + enclitic -ش). از introduces the topics 'about/concerning' and is followed by a coordination خواب و خورد 'sleep and eating/food' as the objects of inquiry. Word order is Subject + Verb(+object enclitic) + از + topic(s).
segments.s497-g54: This continues the list of things asked about: از آسایش روز 'about the comfort/ease of the day' with از again marking the topic, followed by a coordination ننگ و نبرد 'shame and battle'. The nouns are simply enumerated as objects of بپرسیدش.
segments.s497-g55: ز پوشیدنی و ز گستردنی uses the preposition ز 'regarding/from' twice to list two categories: پوشیدنی ('wearables/garments') and گستردنی ('things to spread', i.e. bedding/coverings). The repetition of ز is a poetic device to balance the coordination.
segments.s497-g56: همه بی‌نیازیم is a predication 'we (are) all self‑sufficient / in no need' where بی‌نیازیم is an adjective with first‑person plural ending. از خوردنی is a prepositional complement 'of/from food(edibles)'; together they mean 'we all have no need of food'.
segments.s497-g57: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو meaning 'when'. The adjective برهنه ('naked') comes before the verb phrase (a typical poetic order), followed by چو + verb زاید ('is born') with the poetic preposition ز ('from') and the source مادر ('mother'). The indefinite subject کسی ('someone/anyone') appears at the end for emphasis.
segments.s497-g58: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one should not/it is not proper'. The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause. نازد is a present verbal form meaning here 'take pride / pamper/boast' (poetic usage). بپوشش is ب‑ (present/subjunctive/infinitive marker) + پوش + ‑ش (3rd‑person object pronoun) = 'to clothe/cover him'. بسی follows as an adverb 'much/abundantly' or 'enough' modifying بپوشش.
segments.s497-g59: وز here is a poetic form (likely a contraction of و ز = 'and from'), introducing the clause. ایدر means 'here'. برهنه شود is a stative/result phrase 'becomes/be laid bare' with شود (3rd‑person singular of شدن). باز is an adverb 'again', and خاک ('earth/soil') is the patient/subject that is described as becoming bare; poetic word order places the predicate before or around the noun.
segments.s497-g60: همه جای is a fixed phrase meaning 'every place' or 'everywhere' (همه modifying جای). ترس است is a nominal predication 'there is fear' (است linking predicate). The following و تیمار و باک list additional abstract nouns ('care' and 'dread'), coordinated with و.
segments.s497-g61: This equational clause places زمین ('the earth') as the subject and names two roles for it: بستر ('bed') و پوشش ('and covering'), followed by the source phrase از آسمان ('from the sky' / 'against the sky'). Word order (noun + predicates + prepositional phrase) is typical in classical Persian poetic description.
segments.s497-g62: به ره here can be read as 'on the path/for the watchman' or as an address toward the watchman (vocative setting). دیده‌بان 'watchman/sentinel' is being addressed or located. تا کی آید زمان is an interrogative temporal clause 'until when will the time come?' with تا کی ('until when') + آید (3rd‑person present 'come') and زمان as the noun 'time/appointed time'.
segments.s497-g63: جهانجوی is a compound noun 'world‑seeker' (one who seeks worldly gain) and is the subject. چندین ('many' / 'several') modifies the verb phrase that follows. بکوشد is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of کوشیدن 'to strive/attempt'. به چیز is a prepositional object 'at things' (i.e., 'strives at many things').
segments.s497-g64: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that / which'. آن چیز 'that thing' is the topic; کوشش نیرزد is an idiomatic negative predication: کوشش ('effort') + نیرزد ('is not worth' — archaic form related to ارزیدن). The final به نیز (literally 'to/for also') functions here as an emphatic particle meaning 'even for that' / 'either', so the clause says the effort is not worth that thing either.
segments.s497-g129: چو دانی که = 'since/when you know that'. چو is the temporal/concessive 'when/since' (classical usage) followed by دانی (2nd-person present of دانستن, 'you know'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. The clause that follows (از مرگ خود چاره نیست) is the content of what is known.
segments.s497-g130: ز پیری بتر نیز پتیاره نیست = a short, somewhat archaic predicate: 'from old age there is no worse ...' (پتیاره is an obsolete/uncertain noun). ز پیری is a prepositional phrase 'from/owing to old age'; بتر is a comparative adjective 'worse'; نیز = 'also/likewise'; پتیاره نیست is the negated predicate 'there is no p.' Note: پتیاره is archaic/uncertain and must be glossed here as 'remedy/aid' in context.
segments.s497-g131: جهان را به کوشش چه جویی همی = 'what are you seeking in the world by effort?'. جهان را is the definite object ('the world' / 'this life') put before the verb for emphasis; به کوشش is an instrumental phrase 'by/with effort' (به + noun). چه is the interrogative 'what', جویی is the present/ habitual verbal form 'you seek' (classical 2nd-person) and همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker emphasizing ongoing action.
segments.s497-g132: گل زهر خیره چه بویی همی = 'what scent does the flower, poisoned and stupefied, still have?'. گل is the head noun 'flower'; زهر qualifies it ('poisoned' / 'of poison'); خیره is a participial/adjectival word describing the flower as stunned/astruck. چه بویی همی is the question 'what scent (does it) still have?', with همی again marking ongoing/state emphasis.
segments.s497-g133: ز تو بازماند همین رنج تو = 'of you will remain only this suffering of yours'. ز تو ('from/of you') marks source; بازماند is a 3rd-person present form 'remains / will remain'; همین 'this very' emphasizes the following noun; رنج تو 'your suffering' places the noun before the possessive pronoun for emphasis (classic word order).
segments.s497-g134: به دشمن رسد کوشش و گنج تو = 'your effort and treasure will reach the enemy.' به دشمن is the prepositional phrase 'to the enemy'; رسد is the verb 'reaches / comes to' (3rd-person); کوشش و گنج تو is the compound subject/object 'your effort and your treasure' placed after the verb (permissible in classical Persian word order).
segments.s497-g135: ز بهر کسان رنج بر تن نهی = 'you put/assume suffering on your body for the sake of others.' ز بهر کسان = 'for the sake of people'; رنج بر تن = 'suffering upon the body'; نهی here is an older/clausal verbal form meaning 'you place/put' (classical idiom). (Note: the dictionary senses shown for نهی are not a good fit for this verbal use; the segment functions as a verb.)
segments.s497-g136: ز کم دانشی باشد و ابلهی = 'it is because of little knowledge and folly.' ز introduces cause ('because of'); کم دانشی is a genitive/construct 'lack/insufficiency of knowledge' (کم = insufficient, دانشی = knowledge); باشد is the (classical) third-person copula 'is' (used here in an explanatory sense); و ابلهی adds 'and foolishness/idiocy' as the second cause.
segments.s497-g73: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed sequence meaning 'thus gave answer' (چنین 'thus' before the verb داد 'gave'); که introduces the reported speech that follows, and ای شهریار is a vocative 'O king' attached to that clause (in older/poetic Persian the particle که may sit directly before a vocative).
segments.s497-g74: تو‌گر is the conditional 'if you' (تو + گر), مرده را is the definite/direct object 'the dead (one)' with object marker را, and بشمری is the present/subjunctive form 'you count' used in the protasis after گر; صدهزار is the numeral complement 'a hundred thousand' (object of بشمری).
segments.s497-g75: ازان ... یکی زنده نیست is a partitive/quantifying expression: ازان 'of those' + صد هزاران 'hundred‑thousands/thousands' followed by یکی 'one' as the subject and زنده نیست 'is not alive' (negative existential). Word order places the quantifier phrase before the predicate.
segments.s497-g76: خنک functions here as 'relief/refreshment' (a predicate noun) and is followed by a relative/clarifying clause آنک در دوزخ افگنده نیست '(one) who has been cast into Hell is not (so)'; آنک is a relative pronoun 'who/that', افگنده is a past participle 'cast' and نیست negates the clause. Together the clause says there is no coolness/comfort for those cast into Hell.
segments.s497-g77: بباید is an impersonal modal 'one must/it is necessary', followed by the object phrase همین زنده را نیز 'this very living one also' (را marks the definite object/topic, نیز 'also'). The final word مرد here is used verbally (poetically) to mean 'die' / 'be made dead' — an archaic/poetic use where a form that looks like the noun may function as a verbal or resultative.
segments.s497-g78: یکی رفت is 'one (person) went/left' (رفت used here with the sense of 'depart/vanish', often euphemistic for 'died'), و links the clauses, and نوبت به دیگر سپرد is a passive/impersonal sequence 'the turn was entrusted/handed to another' (نوبت 'turn', به دیگر 'to another', سپرد 'handed/entrusted').
segments.s497-g79: بپرسید is the past-tense verb 'he asked'. The subordinate clause خشکی فزون‌تر‌گر آب contains خشکی 'dryness' as subject and the comparative فزون‌تر plus the conditional particle گر ('if [it is] more'), with آب 'than water' (or 'upon water') supplying the term of comparison — together: 'if dryness were greater than water' / 'if dryness increased more (relative to water)'.
segments.s497-g80: بتابد is a present/subjunctive verb 'would shine' or 'were to shine', and آفتاب is the noun 'sun' which here appears postposed; بروبر 'directly/upon (face-to-face)' modifies the verb indicating direct shining onto something, and همی is an adverb 'continually/just so' emphasizing the action. The clause thus expresses the hypothetical 'if the sun were to shine directly and continually on the water.'
segments.s497-g121: چنین (thus) is an adverb marking the manner of the reply. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (دادن) and governs the direct object پاسخ ('answer/reply'). ورا is the enclitic prepositional pronoun 'to him' (به + او) marking the indirect object. شهریار is the subject ('the king') placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s497-g122: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). بامرگ (با + مرگ) is a prepositional phrase 'with/in the face of death'. خواهش is a noun 'request/plea' and نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive form نـ + آید meaning 'does not come / does not avail'. به کار here is an idiomatic object: 'به کار آمدن' = 'to be of use', so the clause says 'pleas are of no use.'
segments.s497-g123: چه is an interrogative/exclamatory particle here meaning 'what (use)' or 'what is the point of'. پرهیزی is a verbal noun meaning 'an avoidance/abstention' and is governed by the preposition از 'from', which introduces the thing avoided. تیز modifies چنگ (sharp claw), and اژدها follows as a genitival noun: 'the dragon's claw.'
segments.s497-g124: که here gives a causal/concessive clause ('for/since'). گرزآهنی is the noun 'iron mace' (the thing expected to help). ز + و is the poetic contraction meaning 'from it / from him' (i.e. 'from the dragon'); نیابی is the negative present 'you will not find' (نـ + یابی), and رها functions as a participial/attributive word 'that frees' or 'to free', so the sense is 'you will not find an iron mace that frees you from it.'
segments.s497-g125: جوانی is an indefinite noun 'a youth / a young man.' که introduces a relative clause 'who'. آید is the verb 'comes' (present/habitual here, typical of epic style). بمابر is a compound adverbial phrase 'به ما بر' = 'to us / before us' (i.e. 'comes to our presence'), and دراز is an adjective 'long/tall' describing the youth.
segments.s497-g126: هم before the prepositional phrase has the sense 'even' (emphatic). از روز پیری literally 'from the day of old age' = 'when old age comes.' نیابد is the negative present form 'he does not find/obtain' and جواز is the direct object meaning 'permission/justification/exemption.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetry, but the clause means he will find no permission when old age arrives.
segments.s497-g127: برهمن is the agent 'the Brahmin' (a wise/ascetic man). بدو is 'to him' (به + او) marking the indirect object. گفت is the past tense 'said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and پادشا is 'king' (vocative). The clause is the opening of a direct address.
segments.s497-g128: جهاندار, دانا, فرمانروا are a string of epithets in the vocative: 'lord/holder of the world', 'wise', 'ruler/sovereign'. The coordinating و simply links the adjectives; all three describe the addressee (the king) and continue the Brahmin's address.
segments.s497-g41: سکندر (subject) + چو (temporal conjunction) + روی برهمن (object) + بدید (verb, past). چو here means "when." The verb بدید is past tense "saw" placed at the end as is normal in Persian prose/poetry; روی برهمن is a noun phrase "the face of the Brahmin."
segments.s497-g42: بران گونه is a fixed-like adverbial phrase "in that manner/so," with بران (archaic) marking 'there/on that' and گونه 'manner.' آواز ایشان is a genitive phrase "their voice/voices" (ایشان = they/them). شنید is the past tense "(he) heard," completing the clause.
segments.s497-g43: دوان is a verbal/participial form meaning "running/rushed" (used as an action describing them). The following coordinated nouns "برهنه تن و پای و سر" list parts of the person (body, feet, head) with برهنه applying to the whole list: 'naked in body, feet, and head.' Word order places the action before the descriptive noun phrase.
segments.s497-g44: تنان is the plural noun "bodies/persons." بی‌بر is an adjectival phrase "without offspring/childless" modifying تنان. The conjunction و links جان ("life/soul") as a second predicate; ز دانش به بر uses ز to mark source "from/through knowledge" and به بر (a poetic locution) to indicate bearing or embracing knowledge—together giving the sense "their lives were given to/occupied with knowledge." The copula is omitted (typical in Persian), so adjectives and phrases are predicative.
segments.s497-g45: ز introduces origin "from/of." برگ گیا پوشش is a noun phrase "a covering of plant leaves" (گیا = plant). The following از تخم خورد is a verbal expression: خورد here is a poetic past meaning "was produced/grew" (literally 'took/from seed happened'), so the whole means "the covering of plant leaves sprang from seed."
segments.s497-g46: برآسوده is a predicative adjective meaning "relieved/at ease." The phrase از رزم و روز نبرد uses از to mark separation/absence "from war and the days of battle," so the clause says they were at ease, free from fighting; the two nouns رزم and نبرد are coordinated for emphasis.
segments.s497-g47: خور و خواب و آرام is a triad of activities/states: خور here functions as a nominalized verbal root meaning "eating/food," خواب "sleep," آرام "rest/peace." بر دشت و کوه places these actions spatially: 'on the plain and (on) the mountain.' The preposition بر governs the coordinated locations.
segments.s497-g48: برهنه (adjective) modifies گروه (the noun 'company/troop'). به هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'to/into every place.' گشته is the perfect participle (or past participle used intransitively) meaning 'has gone/was gone'—so 'the company, naked, had gone to every place.' The participle comes before or after its noun in poetry; here the participle predicates movement of the group.
segments.s497-g17: چه داری is an interrogative phrase: چه (what) + داری (present tense, 2nd-person singular of داشتن) — 'what do you have' / 'what business have you'. بدین is classical condensed 'به + این' (to/at this) and introduces the locative phrase بدین مرز ('at this limit/boundary'). بی‌ارز رای is a noun phrase where بی‌ارز (worthless) modifies رای (opinion/counsel); word order is modifier before head, so 'the worthless counsel'.
segments.s497-g18: نشست here functions as a past action ('sat' / 'were seated' / 'assembled') rather than the nominal 'session' (classical Persian often uses a s-form verb before its subject). پرستندگان خدای is the subject phrase 'the worshippers/devotees of God' (پرستندگان = worshippers; خدای = God in genitive relation). The verb precedes the subject, a typical poetic/classical word order.
segments.s497-g19: گرین آمدنت is a noun phrase: گرین (tearful/sorrowful) modifies آمدنت (your coming — آمدن + enclitic -ت). از پی is a fixed classical sequence meaning 'after / at the request of / in pursuit of', and خواسته‌ست is a perfect construction ('has been desired/asked for'). The verb خواسته‌ست governs the preceding nominal phrase, so the clause means 'your tearful coming has been desired/asked for'.
segments.s497-g20: خرد is the subject 'wisdom'. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. نزد تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in your presence' or 'before you' (نزد + pronoun). کاسته‌ست is a perfect/passive-stative form 'has diminished / is diminished' (کاستن), so the clause reads 'wisdom has doubtless diminished in your presence.'
segments.s497-g21: بر ما ... است is an idiomatic classical/periphrastic construction meaning 'for us there is ...' i.e. 'we have ...'. شکیبایی و دانش is a coordinated nominal predicate ('patience and knowledge'), and است is the copula placed after the predicate, yielding 'we possess patience and knowledge.'
segments.s497-g22: ز دانش is an ablative/instrumental prepositional phrase 'from/by virtue of knowledge'. روانها (souls) is the grammatical subject, followed by the predicate 'پر از رامش است' where پر (full) takes an of-phrase introduced by از, so 'souls are full of tranquillity' — overall: 'from knowledge, souls are filled with calm.'
segments.s497-g23: شکیبایی از ما نشاید ستد is a negative impersonal/modal clause: نشاید ('it is not fit/should not') + ستد ('to take/exact') yields 'it is not fitting that patience be taken from us' (شکیبایی = patience; از ما = from us). The verb combination expresses prohibition or impropriety rather than a simple factual past.
segments.s497-g24: نه کس را is a negative focus phrase 'no one' (نه + کس) with the object/contrast marker را. ز دانش is 'through/from knowledge'. رسد is a present/optative-classical form 'comes to / befalls / attains', and بد is the predicate 'bad/ill'. The clause means 'nor will anyone, by means of knowledge, come to ill (suffer harm) likewise.' Word order places the negator before کس and the verb toward the end, typical of classical style.
segments.s497-g97: روان ترا = 'your soul': the possessive is formed with the enclitic ت (here written separately) plus the object/topic marker را (classical orthography produces ترا). دوزخ است آرزوی is a poetic inversion where آرزوی (the subject 'desire/wish') is linked to the predicate دوزخ ('hell') by the copula است, so literally 'desire is hell.'
segments.s497-g98: مگر = 'unless' (conditional particle). زین is a poetic/contractional form here meaning 'from this' (equivalent to از این) and links to سخن 'this speech/these words.' بازگردی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive (poetic) meaning 'you return/turn back' (the -ی ending marks 2nd person). به خوی = 'to your nature/disposition' with به introducing the target and خوی meaning 'disposition/habit.'
segments.s497-g99: دگر گفت = 'another (one) said' (دگر = 'another'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' — note the provided sense list does not show the verbal sense here). بر جان ما is an oath formula 'by our life' (بر + جان with ما as possessor). شاه کیست is an interrogative inversion: شاه (the noun) followed by کیست ('who is'), i.e. 'who is the king?'.
segments.s497-g100: به کژی = 'to/for crookedness/wrongness' with به marking relation and کژی 'crookedness, wrongness.' بهر جای here means roughly 'for any place/at every turn' (بهر = 'for/toward', جای = 'place/position', used adverbially). همراه کیست = 'who is companion' (کیست = 'who is'), i.e. 'who is companion in wrongdoing anywhere?'.
segments.s497-g101: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave answer/thus replied' (داد = 'gave' functioning with پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the reported clause. آز است شاه is a poetic inversion: آز ('greed') is predicate-subject linked by است to شاه ('king'), giving the meaning 'that greed is king.'
segments.s497-g102: سر مایه is a nominal compound meaning 'chief/source' (سر in the sense 'chief/top' + مایه 'cause, source'). کین و جای گناه are two coordinated nouns: کین 'hatred/vengeance' and جای گناه 'the place/seat of sin' (جای = 'place/seat', گناه = 'sin'); the whole phrase appositive to 'greed' as its effects/sources.
segments.s497-g103: بپرسید is the past tense 'he asked' (third-person past in classical style). خود گوهر is the emphatic subject 'the jewel itself' (خود = 'self/itself'). از بهر چیست is an interrogative phrase 'for what purpose is it?' where از بهر = 'for the sake of/on account of' and چیست = 'what is (it)'.
segments.s497-g104: This line is somewhat idiomatic/classical: کش here is an archaic/poetic particle whose exact modern equivalent is uncertain in this context (hence marked below as unspecified); از بهر بیشی = 'on account of excess/for additional gain' (از بهر = 'for/on account of', بیشی = 'more/excess'). بباید گریست uses the modal/obligative بباید ('one ought/must') followed by the simple verbal form گریست 'to weep/one should weep' — together 'one ought to weep.'
segments.s497-g1: وزان جایگه = 'و ز آن جایگه' (literary contraction): 'from that place/position'. لشکر اندر کشید is a past action with کشید (past 3rd singular) meaning 'drew/moved' the army; اندر = 'into/in' marks direction or emplacement. Word order is typical older Persian (preposition-like phrase + object + verb).
segments.s497-g2: دمان تا = 'at the skirts/foothill until' where تا here means 'until' marking the extent of movement. به شهر = 'to the city' (به = to/towards) and برهمن is the place-name 'Brahmin' (a town/settlement associated with a Brahmin). رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived'.
segments.s497-g3: بدان = a contracted locative/demonstrative (ب + آن) meaning 'to that/thereupon' (here written as separate parts). تا introduces purpose ('so that/in order that'). ز کردارهای کهن = 'about/from ancient deeds' with ز = 'about/concerning', کردارهای plural 'deeds', کهن 'ancient'.
segments.s497-g4: بپرسد is a 3rd-person form of پرسیدن used here in a purposive/subjunctive sense 'that he might ask' (classical Persian uses this form to express purpose). ز introduces the source/target of the question 'from/about', پرهیزگاران is the plural noun 'the pious' (those who abstain), and سخن here means 'word/speech/matter' (i.e. 'ask about the matter').
segments.s497-g5: برهمن چو آگه شد = 'When the Brahmin became aware' with چو = 'when' and آگه شد 'became aware' (شد = past of شدن 'to become'). از کار شاه = 'of/regarding the king's affair/deed' where از has the sense 'concerning' and کار here = 'deed/affair'.
segments.s497-g6: که آورد = relative/causal 'which/so that he brought/caused'; زان ('from/for that reason') gives the causal link 'therefore'; روی لشگر به راه literally 'the army onto the road' with روی = 'upon/on' and به راه = 'on the way/marched'. آورد is past 'he brought/caused (to move)'.
segments.s497-g7: پرستنده modifies مرد as a participial adjective 'a worshipping/devout man'. اندر آمد = 'came in/entered' (آمد = past 'came', اندر = 'into/in'). ز کوه = 'from the mountain' (ز = from). Word order places the modifier before the noun, a common classical pattern.
segments.s497-g8: شدند = past 3rd-person plural 'they became' or 'it happened to them'; اندران = 'among them/therein' (locative), آگهی = 'news/information' (the item they became aware of), and همگروه = 'companions/fellow-members' indicating who became informed. Together: 'among them the companions were made aware.'
segments.s497-g33: سپه را سراسر هم آنجا بماند = literal word order is object + adverbials + verb. سپه را (army + object marker) places 'the army' as the definite thing affected; سراسر هم (sara-sar + ham) stresses that it was the entire army, with هم adding an 'also/indeed' emphasis; آنجا binds the location 'there'. بماند is a third-person narrative form of ماندن ('remain/leave'), so the clause means 'he left the whole army there' or 'the army remained there'.
segments.s497-g34: خود و فیلسوفان رومی براند = a compound subject ('he himself and the Byzantine philosophers') followed by براند. The verb has the prefixed ب‑ typical of older/classical present/ narrative forms (here rendered as 'rode/continued'); رومی is used of Rûm/Byzantine people. The clause therefore says they set out/rode on.
segments.s497-g35: پرستنده آگه شد از کار شاه = subject (پرستنده, 'the worshippers') + predicate آگه شد ('became aware, learned'). از introduces the source/content ('of/from the king's affair/deed'). This is a common Persian expression: آگه شدن از X = 'to hear about/learn of X'.
segments.s497-g36: پذیره شدندش یکایک به راه = پذیره ('reception/acceptance') plus شدندش (شدند + enclitic ش = 'they became / they took him') is an idiomatic older construction meaning 'they received/took him in'; the enclitic ش refers to the person received ('him'). یکایک 'one by one' modifies the action, and به راه 'on the road / along the way' gives the location/direction.
segments.s497-g37: ببردند بی‌مایه چیزی که بود = ببردند is the past/pluperfect narrative 'they took (away)'; بی‌مایه ('without substance' = 'paltry/worthless') modifies چیزی ('something/whatever'); که بود is a relative clause 'that there was' — together: 'they took whatever paltry things there were.'
segments.s497-g38: که نه گنج بدشان نه کشت و درود = the clause gives the reason/contrast: 'for they had neither treasure nor crops nor offerings/greetings.' In classical usage an elided/copied copula appears here (بدشان is an archaic contracted form related to 'بودشان' = 'they had'); نه ... نه ... enumerates what they lacked (گنج, کشت, درود).
segments.s497-g39: یکایک برو خواندند آفرین = یکایک 'one by one'; برو here carries the sense 'as they went/going on' (derived from the verb برو = 'go'), so the sequence means 'one by one, as they went, they called/uttered'. خواندند is used here in the sense 'uttered/called out' and آفرین is the acclamation 'Bravo!/Well done!'.
segments.s497-g40: بران برمنش شهریار زمین = بران ('upon that' / 'to him on that [occasion]') is a demonstrative/adverbial reference to the person just praised; برمنش 'noble‑minded' qualifies شهریار ('king/sovereign'); زمین here means 'the land' or 'realm' (common epithet: 'king of the land'). The whole phrase is the indirect object/goal of the acclamation: 'to that noble-minded king of the land.'
segments.s497-g105: چنین (thus) is an adverb modifying the whole speech act. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (here 'gave an answer'); پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. که introduces the content clause ('that...') and links to the following noun phrase آز و نیاز ('greed and need') which states what the answer is about.
segments.s497-g106: دو (two) quantifies the following noun; دیوند here is used as a substantive meaning a demonic or demon‑like being. بیچاره is an adjectival/nominal epithet 'wretched/poor fellow' attached to that noun. و simply coordinates with دیوساز, a compound meaning roughly 'demon‑maker' or 'one who creates/devilish trouble', so the phrase characterises the pair as two miserable, devilish creatures.
segments.s497-g107: یکی را marks 'one (of them)' with را as a topical/object marker. ز کمی (from lack/for want) gives the cause; شده خشک لب is a compound predicate: شده 'has become' + خشک لب 'dry‑lipped' (literally 'dry lip'), so the clause says that one has become dry‑lipped because of scarcity.
segments.s497-g108: یکی introduces 'the other one'. از here carries a causal sense ('because of' / 'from'), followed by فزونیست, a poetic contracted form meaning something like '(is) from excess/abundance' or 'due to abundance'. بی‌خواب شب is an adjectival phrase 'night‑sleepless' (sleepless at night). The whole clause states that the other is sleepless at night because of excess.
segments.s497-g109: همان 'that very' focuses the time; هر دو را is the object 'both of them' (هر here functioning with دو to mean 'both'). روز می‌بشکرد uses the imperfective/poetic verb می‌بشکرد (literally 'would split/cleave') employed figuratively — here meaning that the daytime (or the day) would 'reveal' or 'bring to light' them both. Word order is topical/time-marker + object + verb.
segments.s497-g110: خنک is the predicate noun 'the coolness' or 'refreshment'. آنک is an archaic relative pronoun 'that which/which', introducing the relative clause جانش پذیرد — جان + ـش (his soul) + پذیرد 'accepts' (present tense used here as a general/habitual truth). خرد ('wisdom') stands appositionally as the identity of that coolness: 'the coolness which his soul accepts is wisdom.'
segments.s497-g111: سکندر is the subject; چو is the conjunction 'when'; گفتار ایشان is the object phrase 'their speech/words' (ایشان = 'their/they' polite/archaic). شنید is simple past 'heard'. The clause is a straightforward temporal 'when X heard Y' construction with verb-final order typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s497-g112: به رخساره شد is an intransitive stative construction meaning 'became upon the face' (i.e., 'his expression became...'); شد is the past of 'become'. چون introduces a simile 'like', گل is 'flower', and شنبلید is the specific floral image (here the fenugreek/flowering ear). Together: 'his face became like a fenugreek flower' (a poetic simile describing a change of complexion/appearance).
segments.s497-g89: Literal order: adjective-comparative + noun + possessor + copula. گنهکارتر is the comparative adjective 'more sinful/blameworthy' modifying چیز 'thing'; مردم follows as the possessor/host ('of the people' / 'among people'); بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s497-g90: که introduces an explanatory relative/clause ('that'); از here marks cause ('because of' / 'on account of') governing the coordinated nouns کین و آزش 'hatred and greed'. خرد is the subject 'wisdom/reason' and گم بود is a two-word predicate 'was lost' (گم used adjectivally + بود).
segments.s497-g91: چو + خواهی sets a conditional 'if you want'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that you may know this'. این را is the definite object (را marks it), and بدانی is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'to know' used after که. درست functions as an adverb 'correctly/rightly' qualifying the verb.
segments.s497-g92: تن خویشتن را is an emphatic object phrase 'your own person/self' (را marks it). نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (keeping/watch) + کن (imperative 'do') = 'keep/watch (over)'. نخست is an adverb 'first', placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s497-g93: که introduces the explanatory clause. روی زمین 'on the face/surface of the earth' is a circumstantial phrase; سربسر is an adverb 'throughout / entirely'; پیش here means 'in the presence of / before (others)'. تست is an archaic copular form (functioning as 'are' in classical Persian) completing the clause.
segments.s497-g94: تو گویی is a parenthetical 'you would say' (گویی = 'you say / you would say'). سپهر روان خویش is a possessive phrase 'the heavens/sky of your soul' (خویش formed from خوی + ش = 'one's own'), and the final تست is again the archaic copula 'is' inside the reported clause. Together: 'you would say the heavens are your spirit.'
segments.s497-g95: همی adds emphatic/continuous sense ('you even / you continually'). رای داری is an idiom 'you hold an opinion / you intend' (داري = you have). که introduces the content of that opinion; افزون کنی is a subjunctive infinitival idea 'that you may add / increase / make greater' with کنی as the subjunctive verb.
segments.s497-g96: ز خاک ... بیرون کنی is a purposive/result clause 'that you may bring out from the earth'. ز خاک 'from the earth/soil' + سیه مغز 'black brain' (adjective + noun), and بیرون کنی is the verb phrase 'bring out / extract' with کنی in the subjunctive following که.
segments.s497-g9: نوشتند is the simple past 3rd-person-plural 'they wrote'. پس is an adverb 'then/thereupon' linking this action to what came before. نامه‌ای is an indefinite direct object 'a letter', and بخردان is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to the wise' (poetic contraction of به خردان), placed after the object for rhythmic/poetic word order.
segments.s497-g10: به نزد is a fixed poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'to the presence of / to' (motion toward someone). سکندر is the addressee's name; سر موبدان is an appositive/title where سر 'head/chief' (here index 2) modifies موبدان 'the mobeds/priests'.
segments.s497-g11: سر نامه = 'the head/top of the letter' (سر here 'top, heading'). بود is the past copula 'was'. آفرین functions as a noun 'praise/acclamation', and نهان is an adjective 'hidden/secret' modifying that praise; the phrase gives the contents of the letter's heading.
segments.s497-g12: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. داننده 'the knower/expert' is the source/author of the praise. بر here functions as a poetic preposition 'toward/to' governing شهریار جهان 'the king (ruler) of the world'—i.e. the praise is addressed to the sovereign of the world.
segments.s497-g13: که introduces the wish/clause 'that...'. پیروزگر is the predicative adjective 'victorious'. باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical/poetic use) that gives the sense 'may X be Y'. همواره 'always' is an adverb modifying the wish, and شاه is the subject 'the king'—word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s497-g14: به introduces a list of blessings/favors 'in/with'. افزایش 'increase' (prosperity), دانش 'knowledge', and دستگاه 'the court/establishment' are coordinated by و; the whole phrase specifies the domains in which the wished victory/endurance should obtain.
segments.s497-g15: دگر is an adverb 'again' (introducing another speech). گفت is the past verb 'he said' (the provided sense list does not include this verbal sense). کای is an archaic/vocative particle 'O', شهریار is 'king', and سترگ is an epithet 'great' (here used adjectivally; the provided sense list does not match this meaning).
segments.s497-g16: ترا (written here split as ت + را) is the object clitic 'you' (ت + object marker را). داد is the past transitive 'gave'. یزدان is the subject 'God', and جهان بزرگ is the noun phrase 'the great world'—the thing given; overall: 'God gave you the great world.'
